pronunciation: /əˈdrɔɪtli/ — stress on the second syllable: *a-DROIT-ly*. The *oi* sounds like the vowel in *boy* or *coin*. A common slip is to stress the first syllable (*AD-roit-ly*) — avoid that.
definition: Adroitly is the adverb form of adroit — it describes doing something with impressive skill, cleverness, and ease, particularly in situations that demand quick thinking or deft handling. The word carries a dual quality: it can refer to physical nimbleness (catching something before it falls) as well as mental sharpness (steering a tricky conversation). It comes from the French à droit, literally "to the right", since right-handedness was historically associated with skill and correctness.
examples: She adroitly steered the conversation away from the uncomfortable topic.
The diplomat adroitly navigated the competing interests of both parties.
He adroitly caught the falling glass before it hit the floor.

pronunciation: /kənˈfaɪnd/ — stress on the second syllable: *con-FINED*. The *i* in *fined* is the long vowel, as in *mind* or *kind*. The first syllable is unstressed and reduced: *kən-*, not *kon-*.
definition: Confined works as both an adjective and the past tense/participle of the verb to confine. As an adjective, it means restricted in space, movement, or scope — the situation allows little room, physical or figurative. It often carries a sense of discomfort or inadequacy: the space or role is smaller than it should be. As a verb (to confine), it means to restrict or keep within boundaries — a person to a place, a discussion to a topic, a fire to a floor. The dominant collocation is confined to: a p
examples: Working in such a confined space made the job twice as exhausting. *(adjective)*
The patient was confined to bed for two weeks after the surgery. *(verb)*
Please confine your remarks to the matter at hand. *(verb)*

pronunciation: /ˈdɛnsli/ — *DENS-lee*. The *e* is short, as in *ten*. One compressed syllable, then *-ly*. No traps.
definition: Densely is the adverb form of dense — meaning in a way that is closely packed, thick, or concentrated. It can describe physical crowding (densely forested), information load (densely written), or population (densely populated). The base adjective dense does extra work: it covers physical thickness (dense fog), intellectual difficulty (dense prose), and informally, stupidity (don't be dense). Densely inherits all of these except the last, which stays adjectival. From Latin densus — thick, crowded
examples: The forest was so densely packed with trees that no light reached the ground.
The report was densely written — every sentence carried three ideas at once.
The city centre is densely populated, with over 15,000 people per square kilometre.

pronunciation: /ɪmˈbɑːrk/ — stress on the second syllable: *em-BARK*. The *ar* is the same sound as in *park* or *start*. Don't soften it to *em-BERK* — keep the open *ah* vowel.
definition: Embark is a verb meaning to begin a significant journey, undertaking, or new venture. The word almost always implies something momentous — one does not embark on a quick errand, but on an expedition, a career, or a project. A sense of deliberate departure is baked into its meaning. Literally it means to board a ship, from French embarquer (barque = boat). That nautical origin still echoes in figurative use: there is always a feeling of leaving something behind and heading into open water. The wo
examples: After years of planning, they finally embarked on the overland journey to Mongolia.
She embarked on a new career in architecture at the age of forty.
The company is about to embark on its most ambitious expansion yet.

pronunciation: /ɪnˈrɪtʃɪŋ/ — stress on the second syllable: *en-RICH-ing*. The *ch* sounds like in *church*. Three syllables, cleanly separated: *en-RICH-ing*.
definition: Enriching is the present participle of to enrich, used as an adjective meaning something that makes you richer — not financially, but in knowledge, experience, capability, or depth as a person. It implies that what was added had genuine, lasting value rather than mere novelty. It sits in a cluster of words describing experiences that improve you. The differences are subtle but real: enriching is the most balanced and natural of the group — it says something made you more, without specifying exac
examples: Living abroad for a year was one of the most enriching experiences of my life.
I find our conversations genuinely enriching — I always leave with something I didn't have before.
The course was not just useful but enriching in a way I hadn't expected.

pronunciation: *Enthusiasm* — /ɪnˈθjuːziæzəm/ — *en-THYOO-zi-az-um*, five syllables, stress on the second. The *th* is soft as in *think*. *Spike* — /spaɪk/ — one syllable, rhymes with *like*. *Abandonment* — /əˈbændənmənt/ — *a-BAN-don-ment*, stress on the second syllable.
definition: Enthusiasm spike followed by abandonment describes a pattern almost everyone recognises: a new project, habit, or goal begins with an intense burst of energy and engagement — then stalls, fades, and is quietly dropped. The spike feels like progress but is actually just novelty. When the novelty wears off, the underlying difficulty remains, and without structure to compensate, the whole thing collapses. The phrase is not a fixed technical term but a vivid description of a well-documented phenomen
examples: He launched three newsletters last year — each with an enthusiasm spike followed by abandonment after the third issue.
The gym in January is a perfect illustration: enthusiasm spike in week one, abandonment by week three.
She recognised the pattern early and deliberately set a low bar — one sentence a day — to avoid the usual enthusiasm spike followed by abandonment.

pronunciation: /ˈhaɪndsaɪt/ — stress on the first syllable: *HIND-sight*. Two clear syllables; the *hind* rhymes with *mind* or *kind*. No silent letters, no traps.
definition: Hindsight is a noun meaning the ability to understand or evaluate a situation clearly only after it has happened. You couldn't see it at the time — but looking back, it's obvious. It almost always appears in the fixed phrase in hindsight (or with hindsight), which signals that what follows is a retrospective judgement the speaker couldn't have made in the moment. The word belongs to a neat family of English -sight compounds, each pairing sight with a directional prefix: fore- (before), hind- (be
examples: In hindsight, taking that job was the best decision I ever made.
It's easy to criticise the strategy in hindsight — nobody saw the crisis coming.
She admitted, with hindsight, that she had moved too fast.

pronunciation: /ˈnɔːtɪkəl/ — stress on the first syllable: *NAW-ti-kul*. Three syllables. The *au* sounds like the vowel in *law* or *thought*. A common error is to insert an *r*: *NAR-ti-kul* — there is no *r*.
definition: Nautical is an adjective meaning relating to sailors, ships, or navigation at sea. It is the go-to word for anything belonging to the world of seafaring — instruments, terminology, tradition, aesthetic. It sits in a cluster of sea-related adjectives, each with a slightly different register: nautical is the broadest and most everyday; naval is specifically military; maritime is formal and legal; marine shades biological or military. The root is Greek nautes (sailor), from naus (ship) — the same l
examples: The apartment was decorated in a nautical style — rope coils, charts, and a brass compass on the shelf.
A nautical mile is slightly longer than a standard mile, originally defined by the Earth's geometry.
He peppered his speech with nautical metaphors, forever talking about headwinds and staying the course.

pronunciation: /ˈnʌdʒɪŋ/ — *NUJ-ing*. The *dg* makes a soft *j* sound, as in *judge* or *bridge*. A common error is to harden it: *NUG-ing* — avoid that. One syllable plus *-ing*.
definition: Nudging is the present participle of to nudge — to push someone gently in a direction, physically or figuratively. The key quality is subtlety: a nudge is not a shove. It suggests, reminds, tilts — it does not command. Used figuratively, it describes any gentle, indirect steering of someone's behaviour or thinking. In behavioural economics, nudging became a technical term: designing choices or environments so that people are steered toward better decisions without removing their freedom. Richard
examples: She nudged him towards applying for the promotion without ever saying it directly.
The app nudges users to drink more water by sending gentle reminders at intervals.
Sometimes a well-timed question is more effective than direct advice — it nudges rather than pushes.

pronunciation: /ˈpepər/ — stress on the first syllable: *PEP-per*. Both *e* vowels are short, as in *bed*. The double *p* means the first syllable closes sharply — *PEP-*, not *PEE-per*.
definition: To pepper something with something means to scatter many instances of something throughout a larger thing — densely, repeatedly, conspicuously. Like actual pepper on food: distributed all over, impossible to miss. It is almost always used figuratively, and carries a faint critical edge. Peppering implies excess, or at least conspicuousness — you notice when someone does it. The pattern is fixed: pepper X with Y, where X is the thing being peppered and Y is what is scattered through it. The word
examples: He peppered his presentation with buzzwords that obscured more than they revealed.
The letter was peppered with exclamation marks, which made it feel frantic.
She peppered her conversation with references to her travels — charming at first, grating by the end.

pronunciation: /ˈʌtər/ — stress on the first syllable: *UT-ter*. The *u* is the short vowel in *cup* or *but*, not the *oo* in *put*. Both syllables are clear: *UT-ter*, not *uh-ter*.
definition: Utter is one of those satisfying English words that pulls double duty as both adjective and verb, with meanings that feel almost unrelated. As an adjective, it means complete or absolute — always intensifying something negative or extreme. It is slightly more formal and literary than complete or total, which gives it a punchy quality when dropped into plain speech: "utter chaos" hits harder than "complete chaos". As a verb, it means to say something, produce a sound, or give voice to something.
examples: The meeting was an utter waste of time. *(adjective)*
She didn't utter a single word throughout the entire journey. *(verb)*
He uttered a low groan when he saw the bill. *(verb)*

pronunciation: /ɪmˈpeə.mənt/ — im-PAIR-ment. Three syllables, stress on the second. The *-air-* sounds like the word *air*. Don't say *IM-pair-ment*.
definition: An impairment is a reduction or loss of function — physical, cognitive, or sensory. It describes a state where something that should work properly is working less well, or not at all. The word sits at a slightly formal, clinical register — you'll find it in medical reports, legal documents, and official contexts more than in casual conversation. A few important distinctions: - impairment — the condition itself (the reduced function)
examples: The accident left him with a permanent impairment of his left hand.
Alcohol causes significant impairment of reaction time and judgement.
The programme was designed to support people living with cognitive impairment.

pronunciation: /ˈskævɪndʒɪŋ/ — stress on the first syllable: *SCAV-en-ging*. The *sc* is a hard /sk/ sound (like *skip*). Three syllables: *SCAV-en-jing*. A common slip is to add a fourth syllable — *SCAV-en-a-ging* — avoid that.
definition: Scavenging is the gerund/present participle of to scavenge — the act of searching through discarded, leftover, or waste material to find something still useful. It carries a gritty, survivalist connotation: it implies necessity or resourcefulness rather than casual looking. Animals scavenge for food (vultures, raccoons, hyenas); humans scavenge at junkyards, in rubble, at flea markets, or in skips. Figuratively, you can scavenge for ideas, parts, time, or scraps of useful information — anywhere
examples: After the festival, locals were scavenging the site for anything left behind.
He spent the afternoon scavenging spare parts from old machines in the garage.
The startup was essentially scavenging talent from companies that had just folded.

pronunciation: /ˈsɒl.ɪ.tjuːd/ (British) · /ˈsɑː.lɪ.tuːd/ (American)
definition: Solitude is the state of being alone — but crucially, it carries no negative connotation by default. It is chosen aloneness, often peaceful and restorative. This is the key distinction from loneliness, which implies an unwanted, painful absence of connection. You seek solitude; loneliness finds you. The word suits reflective, literary, and philosophical contexts. Writers, thinkers, and monks retreat into solitude. It is the kind of word that belongs in a diary.
examples: She spent the morning in solitude, watching the city wake up from her window.
He had always needed long stretches of solitude to do his best work.
There is a particular quality to the solitude of early morning — still, unhurried, yours alone.

pronunciation: /ˈspeə.rɪŋ/ — SPAIR-ing. Two syllables, stress on the first. The *-air-* sounds like the word *air*. Don't confuse with *sparring* /ˈspɑː.rɪŋ/ (boxing practice) — different vowel, different word.
definition: Sparing is an adjective meaning using very little of something, being restrained or economical. It implies deliberate restraint — not from scarcity, but from discipline or caution. It almost always appears in the construction sparing with or sparing in, or as part of the adverb sparingly. It carries a slightly positive connotation — being sparing suggests wisdom, not stinginess. A doctor might tell you to be sparing with salt. A writing coach might tell you to use adverbs sparingly. Note the nea
examples: Be sparing with painkillers — they mask symptoms rather than treat the cause.
She was sparing in her praise, which made it all the more meaningful when it came.
Use this spice sparingly; a little goes a long way.

pronunciation: /ˈtɛn.dən/ — TEN-dən. The second syllable is a weak schwa, almost swallowed. Don't say *ten-DON*. The *-on* ending sounds like the *-on* in *button*.
definition: A tendon is a tough, fibrous cord of connective tissue that attaches muscle to bone. When your muscle contracts, it's the tendon that transmits the force and makes the bone move. Tendons are strong but not very elastic — which is exactly why they're vulnerable to injury. The most famous one is the Achilles tendon at the back of the ankle, but you have them all over your body: in your knees (patellar tendon), shoulders (rotator cuff tendons), hands, and feet. Tendons differ from ligaments — ligam
examples: He tore his Achilles tendon during the sprint and needed six months of rehab.
Repetitive overhead movements put a lot of strain on the shoulder tendons.
Tendon injuries heal slowly because tendons have poor blood supply.

pronunciation: /jɜːn/ — rhymes with *learn* and *turn*. One syllable. The *y* sounds like the *y* in *yes*; the vowel is the same as in *her* or *bird*. No tricky consonants — the word is simpler to say than it looks.
definition: To yearn is to feel a deep, persistent longing for something — often something distant, lost, or just out of reach. It is more emotionally intense than want or wish, and it carries an ache rather than just a desire. You yearn for things that matter: a place, a person, a time in your life, a version of yourself. The word has a slightly literary register, which means it feels at home in writing, song lyrics, and serious prose, but it is not out of place in speech when the feeling is genuine.
examples: She yearned for the simplicity of her childhood.
After months abroad, he yearned to hear his own language spoken on the street.
There is something in the music that makes you yearn for places you have never even been.

pronunciation: /MAHn-geh-byur/
/MAHnung/
/erklEHren/
/HIN-tsoo-fyoo-gen/
